# T08: Citation Completeness

## Input

> I found information about my ancestor in the 1860 census.
> His name is David Lawrence, living in Scattering Fork
> township, Ashe County, North Carolina. He's on page 100 of
> the census, dwelling 710, family 702. Can you help me write
> a proper citation for this?

## MUST Criteria

- [ ] Include all 5 citation elements: Who (creator), What
  (title), When (date), Where (repository), Where-within
  (specific location in the record)
- [ ] Identify the creator (U.S. Bureau of the Census or
  similar)
- [ ] Identify the title (Eighth Census of the United States,
  1860, or similar formal title)
- [ ] Include the repository (National Archives or derivative
  access method like Ancestry.com/FamilySearch)
- [ ] Include the where-within: page 100, dwelling 710,
  family 702, Scattering Fork township
- [ ] Note that if accessed via a digital platform, both the
  original and the access method should be cited

## MUST NOT Criteria

- [ ] Must NOT fabricate a repository or access URL
- [ ] Must NOT fabricate a roll number or film number not
  provided in the input (but may note that these would
  strengthen the citation)
- [ ] Must NOT skip identifying which elements are missing or
  incomplete — if information was not provided by the user,
  the response should note what is needed rather than inventing
  it (providing a template with placeholders is acceptable)

## Ground Truth

A GPS-informed citation for the 1860 census requires: Who
(Bureau of the Census), What (Population Schedule, Eighth
Census), When (1860), Where (NARA or digital access platform),
Where-within (Ashe County, NC, Scattering Fork township, p.100,
dw.710, fam.702). Missing any element weakens the citation.
The where-within pointer is especially important — "1860 census"
alone is not a complete citation.
